---
title: "Online Retail Behavioral Intelligence — Technical Analysis"
author: "Ezennia Divine Onyedikachi"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
    number_sections: false
    theme: flatly
    highlight: tango
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# ------------------------------------------------------------
# GLOBAL ENVIRONMENT SETUP
# ------------------------------------------------------------

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE
)

# Load required libraries
library(tidyverse)
library(sf)
library(rnaturalearth)
library(rnaturalearthdata)
library(ggrepel)

# Project working directory note:
# This report assumes execution from project root.
```

# 1. Technical Preface

This document presents the complete technical architecture and analytical execution framework for the **Online Retail Behavioral Intelligence System**.

Unlike static exploratory notebooks, this project is structured as a modular analytical engine composed of sequential scripts (01–11). Each stage produces deterministic artifacts stored as `.rds` objects within the `data/processed/` layer.

The objective of this report is to:

- Document the full analytical pipeline
- Define architectural dependencies
- Ensure reproducibility
- Provide technical transparency
- Establish extensibility for production deployment

This document is intended for:

- Data Scientists  
- Analytics Engineers  
- Technical Leads  
- Business Intelligence Developers  

All insights contained herein are reproducible through deterministic script execution.  

# 2. Project Architecture Overview

## 2.1 Modular Execution Framework

The analytical system follows a strict sequential execution hierarchy:

```
01_data_loading.R
02_data_cleaning.R
03_feature_engineering.R
04_foundational_metrics.R
05_product_performance_analysis.R
06_temporal_dynamics_analysis.R
07_geographic_country_analysis.R
08_cross_dimensional_analysis.R
09_rfm_segmentation_engine.R
10_segment_intelligence_engine.R
11_visualization_engine.R
```

Each script produces structured `.rds` artifacts consumed by downstream stages.

---

## 2.2 Data Layer Separation

The system enforces a three-layer architecture:

**Raw Layer**  
- Original UCI Online Retail dataset  

**Processed Layer**  
- Cleaned datasets  
- Enriched datasets  
- Aggregated intelligence artifacts  

**Visualization Layer**  
- Static PNG outputs  
- Tableau exports  

The processed layer acts as a deterministic checkpoint system enabling modular debugging and reproducibility.

---

## 2.3 Artifact Governance Philosophy

- `.rds` files are excluded from Git tracking.
- All outputs are regenerable via sequential execution.
- No manual transformation occurs outside script logic.
- Each stage operates independently yet remains dependency-aware.

This architecture mirrors enterprise-grade analytics pipeline design.  

# 3. Data Source & Ingestion Layer

## 3.1 Dataset Provenance

The dataset utilized in this analysis is the **UCI Online Retail Dataset**, consisting of transactional records for a UK-based online retail store.

The dataset contains:

- Invoice identifiers
- Product identifiers
- Product descriptions
- Quantities
- Unit prices
- Customer identifiers
- Country information
- Timestamp records

---

## 3.2 Data Loading Procedure

Data ingestion occurs within:

`01_data_loading.R`

The script performs:

- CSV ingestion
- Initial structure validation
- Preliminary column inspection

No transformations are performed at this stage beyond structural validation.  

# 4. Data Cleaning & Structural Normalization

Data cleaning operations are implemented strictly within:

`02_data_cleaning.R`

The cleaning pipeline is deterministic and rule-based.  
No manual preprocessing was performed outside scripted logic.

---

## 4.1 Description Standardization via Lookup Reconstruction

A one-to-one lookup reconstruction was implemented to address missing product descriptions.

Where `Description` values were missing, they were programmatically restored using:

- Unique `StockCode`
- First non-null associated `Description`

This ensures consistency and prevents product fragmentation across records.

---

## 4.2 Missing & Malformed Value Handling

The following normalization rules were enforced:

- Missing `CustomerID` values reassigned to `"Guest"`
- Placeholder `"?"` values converted to `NA`
- Missing `Description` values replaced with `"Missing Description"`
- Whitespace trimmed from `Description` and `Country`
- `Quantity` coerced to integer
- `InvoiceDate` parsed using `mdy_hm()` for timestamp integrity

All transformations were executed using vectorized operations within a single pipeline.

---

## 4.3 Transaction Integrity Enforcement

To preserve transactional validity:

- Invoice numbers were restricted to valid 6-character identifiers
- Negative and zero `Quantity` records were removed
- Duplicate rows were eliminated via `distinct()`

---

## 4.4 Non-Merchandise Transaction Exclusion

The following non-merchandise transaction patterns were excluded:

- POST
- MANUAL
- BANK
- ADJUST
- DISCOUNT
- SAMPLE
- CARRIAGE
- AMAZON
- VOUCHER
- TEST

Filtering was performed using case-insensitive regular expression detection on `Description`.

This ensures the dataset reflects true merchandise-based consumer behavior only.

---

## 4.5 Deterministic Outcome

The output object:

`clean_retail_data`

represents the validated structural foundation for all downstream enrichment and behavioral intelligence stages.

No cleaning logic exists outside this script.  

# 5. Feature Engineering Architecture

The feature engineering layer operationalizes behavioral intelligence by transforming cleaned transactional records into structured analytical signals.

This stage strictly consumes:

`data/processed/online_retail_cleaned.rds`

and produces:

`data/processed/online_retail_enriched.rds`

No external transformations occur outside this defined pipeline.

---

## 5.1 Temporal Signal Construction

Temporal attributes are derived directly from `InvoiceDate` to enable multi-dimensional behavioral analysis.

Engineered features include:

- **Hour** — Hour of transaction occurrence  
- **Day** — Day of week (string format)  
- **WeekPart** — Categorical split (Weekday / Weekend)  
- **Month** — Full month name  

These features enable:

- Hourly demand profiling  
- Daily behavioral dispersion analysis  
- Weekday vs Weekend performance segmentation  
- Seasonal revenue decomposition  

All temporal features are derived using deterministic transformations.

---

## 5.2 Revenue Signal Creation

A transactional revenue metric is created:

Revenue = Quantity × UnitPrice

This transformation enables:

- Monetary aggregation  
- Product revenue ranking  
- Country revenue profiling  
- Segment lifetime value computation  

No revenue normalization or adjustment is applied beyond this direct multiplication.

---

## 5.3 Product Label Normalization Framework

To enable structured product-level intelligence, a controlled normalization strategy is implemented.

The transformation pipeline:

1. Converts descriptions to sentence case  
2. Removes leading and trailing punctuation  
3. Replaces repeated punctuation with spaces  
4. Removes non-informative merchandising words  
5. Removes isolated numeric tokens  
6. Compresses whitespace  

This produces:

`CleanedDesc`

---

## 5.4 Short Product Label Derivation

To ensure aggregation consistency across similar SKUs, a simplified product key is derived:

`ShortProductLabel = first three words of CleanedDesc`

Fallback logic ensures:

- If cleaned label is empty → fallback to original Description  
- If description missing → generate synthetic label using StockCode  

This guarantees:

- No null aggregation keys  
- Deterministic grouping logic  
- Stable cross-dimensional joins  

---

## 5.5 Product Mapping Table Generation

A structured mapping artifact is programmatically generated:

`mapping_table`

Constructed from:

`distinct(ShortProductLabel, Description)`

This table provides:

- Traceability between normalized labels and raw descriptions  
- Audit visibility for product clustering logic  
- Reproducibility validation  

This artifact is stored downstream within:

`product_mapping_table.rds`

---

## 5.6 Architectural Outcome

The feature engineering stage transforms transactional records into a behavioral-ready dataset with:

- Temporal dimensions  
- Revenue intelligence  
- Clean product clustering logic  
- Deterministic aggregation keys  

This enriched dataset becomes the foundational input for:

- Foundational metrics computation  
- Product performance analysis  
- Temporal intelligence modeling  
- Geographic profiling  
- RFM segmentation  
- Cross-dimensional intelligence  

No analytical step bypasses this layer.

This preserves pipeline determinism and architectural integrity.  

# 6. Foundational Metrics Architecture

The foundational metrics layer establishes structural validation over the enriched dataset.

This stage consumes:

`data/processed/online_retail_enriched.rds`

Its objective is not analytical modeling, but **structural verification and dataset integrity confirmation** before higher-order intelligence layers are executed.

No transformations are performed at this stage.
Only validation aggregations.

---

## 6.1 Total Orders (Invoice-Level Validation)

This metric validates the number of distinct transaction invoices within the dataset.

Computed as:

`n_distinct(InvoiceNo)`

Purpose:

- Confirms transaction-level uniqueness
- Validates removal of cancellations
- Ensures invoice integrity post-cleaning
- Serves as denominator for downstream frequency metrics

This represents **transaction throughput volume** at the highest structural level.

---

## 6.2 Total Order Lines (Line-Level Validation)

This metric validates the total number of line-item records.

Computed as:

`n()`

Purpose:

- Confirms row-level integrity
- Validates duplicate removal logic
- Ensures exclusion filters were properly applied
- Provides operational processing scale reference

This represents **granular transactional density**.

---

## 6.3 Total Unique Products (Merchandise Breadth Validation)

This metric validates product diversity after cleaning and normalization.

Computed as:

`n_distinct(StockCode)`

Purpose:

- Confirms successful exclusion of non-merchandise records
- Validates product-level deduplication
- Quantifies merchandise catalog breadth
- Establishes base universe for product intelligence analysis

This represents **catalog dimensionality**.

---

## 6.4 Architectural Role of Foundational Metrics

These metrics serve as:

- Dataset integrity checkpoints
- Baseline denominators for ratio calculations
- Structural validation controls
- Pre-analytics verification layer

No behavioral inference is derived at this stage.

Instead, this layer ensures that all downstream analytical outputs:

- Are computed from validated transactional structures
- Maintain deterministic reproducibility
- Operate on structurally sound data

---

## 6.5 Downstream Dependency

All subsequent analytical layers depend on the structural correctness established here:

- Product Performance Analysis
- Temporal Intelligence Modeling
- Geographic Profiling
- Cross-Dimensional Intelligence
- RFM Segmentation
- Segment Intelligence Engine

This stage confirms that the analytical engine is operating on a validated and stable data foundation.  

# 7. Product Performance Intelligence

This stage transitions from structural validation to merchandise-level intelligence.

It consumes:

`data/processed/online_retail_enriched.rds`

The objective is to evaluate product performance across five analytical dimensions:

- Quantity (Total)
- Quantity (Average per Line)
- Revenue (Total)
- Revenue (Average per Line)
- Order Frequency

Each analytical artifact is persisted using `saveRDS()` to support downstream modular visualization and cross-dimensional modeling.

---

## 7.1 Quantity Performance — Volume-Based

### 7.1.1 Top 10 Products by Total Quantity Sold

Computed as:

`sum(Quantity)`

Grouped by:

- `StockCode`
- `ShortProductLabel`

Purpose:

- Identifies highest unit-movement merchandise
- Reveals bulk-demand dominance
- Informs inventory prioritization
- Highlights volume concentration across catalog

Output Artifact:

`product_top_10_quantity_volume.rds`

---

### 7.1.2 Bottom 10 Products by Total Quantity Sold

Computed identically using:

`sum(Quantity)`

Ordered ascending.

Purpose:

- Identifies lowest-moving inventory
- Detects potential stagnation risk
- Supports assortment rationalization
- Flags products for review or repositioning

Output Artifact:

`product_bottom_10_quantity_volume.rds`

---

## 7.2 Quantity Performance — Average-Based

### 7.2.1 Top 10 Products by Average Quantity per Order Line

Computed as:

`mean(Quantity)`

Purpose:

- Measures purchasing intensity per transaction
- Distinguishes bulk behavior from simple transaction frequency
- Identifies products with high per-line movement
- Supports bundle and pack-size optimization

Output Artifact:

`product_top_10_quantity_average.rds`

---

## 7.3 Revenue Performance — Volume-Based

### 7.3.1 Top 10 Products by Total Revenue Generated

Computed as:

`sum(Revenue)`

Purpose:

- Identifies highest monetary contributors
- Reveals revenue concentration structure
- Guides pricing strategy evaluation
- Supports high-impact merchandising focus

Output Artifact:

`product_top_10_revenue_volume.rds`

---

## 7.4 Revenue Performance — Average-Based

### 7.4.1 Top 10 Products by Average Revenue per Order Line

Computed as:

`mean(Revenue)`

Purpose:

- Evaluates per-transaction monetary efficiency
- Differentiates margin strength from sales volume
- Identifies premium transaction drivers
- Supports strategic product positioning

Output Artifact:

`product_top_10_revenue_average.rds`

---

## 7.5 Order Frequency Performance

### 7.5.1 Top 10 Most Frequently Ordered Products

Computed as:

`n()` per grouped product

Purpose:

- Measures product penetration across processed transactions
- Identifies high-visibility merchandise
- Differentiates behavioral preference from pure volume
- Supports cross-sell and retention strategy

Output Artifact:

`product_top_10_order_frequency.rds`

---

## 7.6 Architectural Role of Product Intelligence Layer

This stage establishes:

- Merchandise dominance hierarchy
- Revenue architecture concentration
- Purchasing intensity structure
- Transaction penetration patterns

It produces reusable artifacts for:

- Temporal cross-analysis
- Geographic dominance mapping
- Segment-level product profiling
- Strategic visualization rendering

All outputs are deterministic and reproducible via pipeline execution.  

# 8. Temporal Behavioral Intelligence

This stage evaluates how transactional behavior distributes across structured time dimensions.

It consumes:

`data/processed/online_retail_enriched.rds`

The objective is to analyze behavioral dispersion across:

- Hour
- Day
- WeekPart
- Month

Each temporal dimension is evaluated across six standardized metrics:

- Total Quantity
- Average Quantity
- Total Revenue
- Average Revenue
- Processed Order Count
- Processed Order Line Count

All artifacts are persisted using `saveRDS()` to maintain modular downstream usability.

---

# 8.1 Hour-Level Analysis

Hour is derived using:

`hour(InvoiceDate)`

This dimension evaluates intra-day behavioral distribution.

---

## 8.1.1 Total Quantity by Hour

Metric:

`sum(Quantity)`

Purpose:

- Identifies peak unit-movement windows
- Detects concentration patterns within the daily cycle
- Supports operational staffing alignment

Output:

`temporal_hour_total_quantity.rds`

---

## 8.1.2 Average Quantity by Hour

Metric:

`mean(Quantity)`

Purpose:

- Measures purchasing intensity per transaction
- Differentiates throughput from basket depth

Output:

`temporal_hour_avg_quantity.rds`

---

## 8.1.3 Processed Order Count by Hour

Metric:

`n_distinct(InvoiceNo)`

Purpose:

- Evaluates transactional throughput
- Identifies busiest order-processing windows

Output:

`temporal_hour_total_orders.rds`

---

## 8.1.4 Processed Order Line Count by Hour

Metric:

`n()`

Purpose:

- Measures transactional density
- Reflects processing workload intensity

Output:

`temporal_hour_total_order_lines.rds`

---

# 8.2 Day-Level Analysis

Day is structured using ordered factor levels:

Sunday → Saturday

This ensures chronological ordering consistency.

---

## 8.2.1 Total Quantity by Day

Metric:

`sum(Quantity)`

Purpose:

- Identifies strongest merchandise movement days
- Detects weekly consumption patterns

Output:

`temporal_day_total_quantity.rds`

---

## 8.2.2 Average Quantity by Day

Metric:

`mean(Quantity)`

Purpose:

- Evaluates purchasing intensity by weekday
- Distinguishes behavioral variation across the weekly cycle

Output:

`temporal_day_avg_quantity.rds`

---

## 8.2.3 Total Revenue by Day

Metric:

`sum(Revenue)`

Purpose:

- Determines highest revenue-generating days
- Reveals monetary concentration patterns

Output:

`temporal_day_total_revenue.rds`

---

## 8.2.4 Average Revenue by Day

Metric:

`mean(Revenue)`

Purpose:

- Measures revenue efficiency per transaction
- Identifies monetization strength across days

Output:

`temporal_day_avg_revenue.rds`

---

## 8.2.5 Processed Order Count by Day

Metric:

`n_distinct(InvoiceNo)`

Purpose:

- Identifies busiest transactional days
- Evaluates throughput intensity

Output:

`temporal_day_total_orders.rds`

---

## 8.2.6 Processed Order Line Count by Day

Metric:

`n()`

Purpose:

- Measures order-line density
- Reflects operational processing load

Output:

`temporal_day_total_order_lines.rds`

---

# 8.3 WeekPart Analysis

WeekPart is derived as:

- Weekday
- Weekend

This enables structural comparison of consumer rhythm.

---

## 8.3.1 Total Quantity by WeekPart

Metric:

`sum(Quantity)`

Purpose:

- Compares merchandise movement between weekday and weekend
- Detects behavioral shift in purchasing rhythm

Output:

`temporal_weekpart_total_quantity.rds`

---

## 8.3.2 Average Quantity by WeekPart

Metric:

`mean(Quantity)`

Purpose:

- Evaluates purchasing intensity across week segments
- Distinguishes transaction depth variation

Output:

`temporal_weekpart_avg_quantity.rds`

---

## 8.3.3 Total Revenue by WeekPart

Metric:

`sum(Revenue)`

Purpose:

- Determines monetary dominance by week segment
- Identifies revenue concentration patterns

Output:

`temporal_weekpart_total_revenue.rds`

---

## 8.3.4 Average Revenue by WeekPart

Metric:

`mean(Revenue)`

Purpose:

- Measures monetization efficiency
- Supports pricing rhythm evaluation

Output:

`temporal_weekpart_avg_revenue.rds`

---

## 8.3.5 Processed Order Count by WeekPart

Metric:

`n_distinct(InvoiceNo)`

Purpose:

- Evaluates transaction throughput by week segment
- Supports capacity planning

Output:

`temporal_weekpart_total_orders.rds`

---

## 8.3.6 Processed Order Line Count by WeekPart

Metric:

`n()`

Purpose:

- Measures transaction density
- Reflects operational intensity distribution

Output:

`temporal_weekpart_total_order_lines.rds`

---

# 8.4 Month-Level Analysis

Month is structured using ordered factor levels:

January → December

This ensures seasonal chronology preservation.

---

## 8.4.1 Total Quantity by Month

Metric:

`sum(Quantity)`

Purpose:

- Identifies seasonal movement patterns
- Detects demand concentration periods

Output:

`temporal_month_total_quantity.rds`

---

## 8.4.2 Average Quantity by Month

Metric:

`mean(Quantity)`

Purpose:

- Evaluates seasonal purchasing intensity
- Differentiates demand rhythm

Output:

`temporal_month_avg_quantity.rds`

---

## 8.4.3 Total Revenue by Month

Metric:

`sum(Revenue)`

Purpose:

- Determines peak revenue seasons
- Reveals monetary concentration trends

Output:

`temporal_month_total_revenue.rds`

---

## 8.4.4 Average Revenue by Month

Metric:

`mean(Revenue)`

Purpose:

- Measures seasonal revenue efficiency
- Supports macro-level pricing evaluation

Output:

`temporal_month_avg_revenue.rds`

---

## 8.4.5 Processed Order Count by Month

Metric:

`n_distinct(InvoiceNo)`

Purpose:

- Evaluates seasonal transaction throughput
- Identifies macro-activity cycles

Output:

`temporal_month_total_orders.rds`

---

## 8.4.6 Processed Order Line Count by Month

Metric:

`n()`

Purpose:

- Measures transaction density across seasonal periods
- Reflects operational processing intensity

Output:

`temporal_month_total_order_lines.rds`

---

# 8.5 Architectural Role of Temporal Intelligence Layer

This stage establishes:

- Intra-day behavioral dispersion
- Weekly rhythm structure
- Weekend vs Weekday contrast
- Seasonal demand cycles
- Revenue concentration timing
- Transaction throughput distribution

It provides the temporal backbone required for:

- Cross-dimensional analysis
- Segment-level behavioral modeling
- Strategic campaign timing
- Operational capacity alignment

All outputs are deterministic and fully reproducible through sequential pipeline execution.  

# 9. Geographic Intelligence Architecture

This stage evaluates transactional and monetary distribution across global markets.

It consumes:

`data/processed/online_retail_enriched.rds`

The objective is to identify:

- Strongest bulk-demand markets
- Revenue-dominant countries
- High-intensity purchasing regions
- Transaction throughput concentration
- Order-line processing density by geography

All outputs are persisted via `saveRDS()` to ensure modular downstream accessibility.

---

# 9.1 Quantity Performance by Country

This layer evaluates merchandise movement across geographic markets.

---

## 9.1.1 Top 10 Countries by Total Purchased Quantity

Metric:

`sum(Quantity)`

Purpose:

- Identifies strongest bulk-demand markets
- Detects global unit-movement concentration
- Reveals high-volume consumption territories

Output:

`country_top_10_total_quantity.rds`

---

## 9.1.2 Top 10 Countries by Average Purchased Quantity

Metric:

`mean(Quantity)`

Purpose:

- Measures per-transaction purchasing intensity
- Identifies markets with deeper basket behavior
- Distinguishes volume dominance from intensity dominance

Output:

`country_top_10_avg_quantity.rds`

---

# 9.2 Revenue Performance by Country

This layer evaluates monetary contribution across global markets.

---

## 9.2.1 Top 10 Countries by Total Generated Revenue

Metric:

`sum(Revenue)`

Purpose:

- Identifies highest revenue-contributing markets
- Reveals monetary concentration globally
- Supports strategic regional prioritization

Output:

`country_top_10_total_revenue.rds`

---

## 9.2.2 Top 10 Countries by Average Revenue

Metric:

`mean(Revenue)`

Purpose:

- Measures revenue intensity per transaction
- Identifies markets with stronger monetization efficiency
- Differentiates scale-driven revenue from efficiency-driven revenue

Output:

`country_top_10_avg_revenue.rds`

---

# 9.3 Order Activity by Country

This layer evaluates transaction throughput and operational density across markets.

---

## 9.3.1 Top 10 Countries by Processed Order Count

Metric:

`n_distinct(InvoiceNo)`

Purpose:

- Evaluates transaction throughput by geography
- Identifies markets with highest order volume
- Supports operational capacity alignment

Output:

`country_top_10_total_orders.rds`

---

## 9.3.2 Top 10 Countries by Processed Order Line Count

Metric:

`n()`

Purpose:

- Measures order-line density
- Reflects processing intensity by region
- Identifies transaction-heavy markets

Output:

`country_top_10_total_order_lines.rds`

---

# 9.4 Architectural Role of Geographic Intelligence Layer

This stage establishes:

- Global demand concentration
- Revenue dominance by country
- Market-level purchasing intensity
- Transaction throughput distribution
- Operational processing load by geography

It forms the geographic backbone required for:

- Cross-dimensional analysis (Product × Country)
- Segment × Geographic modeling
- Strategic regional expansion planning
- Revenue concentration risk assessment

All artifacts are deterministic and reproducible through sequential pipeline execution.  

# 10. Cross-Dimensional Intelligence Architecture

This stage integrates product performance with temporal and geographic dimensions.

It consumes:

`data/processed/online_retail_enriched.rds`

This layer answers:

- Which products dominate specific time windows?
- Which items drive revenue seasonally?
- What products lead transaction processing intensity?
- Which products power top-performing countries?

All outputs are programmatically persisted using `saveRDS()` for deterministic reuse in visualization and segment intelligence stages.

---

# 10.1 Product × Hour Intelligence

Evaluates dominant product behavior within the 24-hour operational cycle.

---

## 10.1.1 Hourly — Quantity (Total)

Metric:

`sum(Quantity)`

Logic:

Top 2 products per hour by total unit demand.

Output:

`cross_product_hour_total_quantity.rds`

Purpose:

Identifies dominant products during peak demand windows.

---

## 10.1.2 Hourly — Quantity (Average)

Metric:

`mean(Quantity)`

Logic:

Top 2 products per hour by purchasing intensity.

Output:

`cross_product_hour_avg_quantity.rds`

Purpose:

Measures hourly purchase depth per product.

---

## 10.1.3 Hourly — Processed Order Line Count

Metric:

`n()`

Logic:

Top 2 products per hour by transactional processing volume.

Output:

`cross_product_hour_order_line_count.rds`

Purpose:

Identifies products generating highest operational load during hourly cycles.

---

# 10.2 Product × Day Intelligence

Evaluates product dominance across ordered weekday structure.

---

## 10.2.1 Daily — Revenue (Total)

Metric:

`sum(Revenue)`

Logic:

Top 2 products per day by total revenue generated.

Output:

`cross_product_day_total_revenue.rds`

Purpose:

Identifies strongest daily monetary contributors.

---

## 10.2.2 Daily — Revenue (Average)

Metric:

`mean(Revenue)`

Logic:

Top 2 products per day by revenue efficiency.

Output:

`cross_product_day_avg_revenue.rds`

Purpose:

Measures revenue performance intensity by day.

---

## 10.2.3 Daily — Processed Order Line Count

Metric:

`n()`

Logic:

Top 2 products per day by order line volume.

Output:

`cross_product_day_order_line_count.rds`

Purpose:

Evaluates daily processing density per product.

---

# 10.3 Product × WeekPart Intelligence

Evaluates product behavior between Weekday and Weekend segments.

---

## 10.3.1 WeekPart — Quantity (Total)

Metric:

`sum(Quantity)`

Logic:

Top 2 products per WeekPart by total unit movement.

Output:

`cross_product_weekpart_total_quantity.rds`

Purpose:

Identifies dominant weekday vs weekend volume drivers.

---

## 10.3.2 WeekPart — Quantity (Average)

Metric:

`mean(Quantity)`

Logic:

Top 2 products per WeekPart by purchasing intensity.

Output:

`cross_product_weekpart_avg_quantity.rds`

Purpose:

Measures product-level purchase depth by week segment.

---

## 10.3.3 WeekPart — Revenue (Total)

Metric:

`sum(Revenue)`

Logic:

Top 2 products per WeekPart by total monetary contribution.

Output:

`cross_product_weekpart_total_revenue.rds`

Purpose:

Identifies weekly revenue leaders.

---

## 10.3.4 WeekPart — Revenue (Average)

Metric:

`mean(Revenue)`

Logic:

Top 2 products per WeekPart by revenue efficiency.

Output:

`cross_product_weekpart_avg_revenue.rds`

Purpose:

Measures monetization strength across week segments.

---

## 10.3.5 WeekPart — Processed Order Line Count

Metric:

`n()`

Logic:

Top 2 products per WeekPart by order line volume.

Output:

`cross_product_weekpart_order_line_count.rds`

Purpose:

Evaluates transaction density across week segments.

---

# 10.4 Product × Month Intelligence

Evaluates seasonal dominance and monetization efficiency.

---

## 10.4.1 Month — Quantity (Total)

Metric:

`sum(Quantity)`

Logic:

Top 2 products per month by unit movement.

Output:

`cross_product_month_total_quantity.rds`

Purpose:

Identifies seasonal demand leaders.

---

## 10.4.2 Month — Quantity (Average)

Metric:

`mean(Quantity)`

Logic:

Top 2 products per month by purchase intensity.

Output:

`cross_product_month_avg_quantity.rds`

Purpose:

Measures seasonal basket depth.

---

## 10.4.3 Month — Revenue (Total)

Metric:

`sum(Revenue)`

Logic:

Top 2 products per month by total revenue generated.

Output:

`cross_product_month_total_revenue.rds`

Purpose:

Identifies seasonal monetary leaders.

---

## 10.4.4 Month — Revenue (Average)

Metric:

`mean(Revenue)`

Logic:

Top 2 products per month by revenue efficiency.

Output:

`cross_product_month_avg_revenue.rds`

Purpose:

Measures seasonal monetization efficiency.

---

## 10.4.5 Month — Processed Order Line Count

Metric:

`n()`

Logic:

Top 2 products per month by order line volume.

Output:

`cross_product_month_order_line_count.rds`

Purpose:

Evaluates seasonal operational processing density.

---

# 10.5 Product × Country Intelligence

Integrates top-performing countries with dominant product drivers.

This layer depends on outputs from Section 9.

---

## 10.5.1 Top Products in Top Quantity Countries (Total Quantity)

Metric:

`sum(Quantity)`

Output:

`cross_country_product_total_quantity.rds`

Purpose:

Identifies products driving unit dominance within highest-demand markets.

---

## 10.5.2 Top Products in Top Quantity Countries (Average Quantity)

Metric:

`mean(Quantity)`

Output:

`cross_country_product_avg_quantity.rds`

Purpose:

Measures product-level purchasing intensity within dominant volume markets.

---

## 10.5.3 Top Products in Top Revenue Countries (Total Revenue)

Metric:

`sum(Revenue)`

Output:

`cross_country_product_total_revenue.rds`

Purpose:

Identifies dominant revenue-driving products within top monetary markets.

---

## 10.5.4 Top Products in Top Revenue Countries (Average Revenue)

Metric:

`mean(Revenue)`

Output:

`cross_country_product_avg_revenue.rds`

Purpose:

Measures product-level monetization efficiency within strongest revenue markets.

---

## 10.5.5 Top Products in Top Order Countries (Order Line Count)

Metric:

`n()`

Output:

`cross_country_product_order_line_count.rds`

Purpose:

Identifies products generating highest transactional processing volume within busiest markets.

---

# 10.6 Architectural Significance

This cross-dimensional layer transforms isolated metrics into:

- Contextual dominance patterns
- Time-bound product leadership
- Seasonal revenue architecture
- Operational load mapping
- Geographic product leverage

It bridges foundational intelligence with behavioral segmentation and advanced customer modeling in subsequent stages.

All outputs are reproducible and modular within the deterministic execution chain.  

# 11. RFM Segmentation Engine

This stage operationalizes customer behavioral classification using
Recency–Frequency–Monetary (RFM) modeling.

It consumes:

`data/processed/online_retail_enriched.rds`

It excludes:

`CustomerID == "Guest"`

This layer transforms transactional history into actionable behavioral segments.

All outputs are programmatically persisted using `saveRDS()`.

---

# 11.1 RFM Metric Computation

This phase constructs the foundational behavioral metrics required for segmentation.

---

## 11.1.1 Recency

Definition:

Days since a customer's most recent purchase relative to the dataset’s maximum transaction date.

Logic:

`max(InvoiceDate) - max(InvoiceDate by CustomerID)`

Interpretation:

Lower values indicate more recent engagement.

---

## 11.1.2 Frequency

Definition:

Total number of distinct invoices per customer.

Logic:

`n_distinct(InvoiceNo)`

Interpretation:

Higher values indicate stronger purchase repetition.

---

## 11.1.3 Monetary

Definition:

Total revenue generated by each customer.

Logic:

`sum(Revenue)`

Interpretation:

Higher values indicate stronger monetary contribution.

---

## Output Artifact

`rfm_base_metrics.rds`

This artifact contains:

- CustomerID
- Recency
- Frequency
- Monetary

Purpose:

Provides the raw behavioral backbone for standardized scoring.

---

# 11.2 RFM Scoring & Segment Classification

This phase converts raw behavioral metrics into standardized quintile scores
and maps customers into strategic behavioral segments.

---

## 11.2.1 Quintile Scoring Logic

Scoring uses `ntile()` to divide customers into five equal groups.

### Recency Score (RScore)

`ntile(-Recency, 5)`

Inverted to reward lower recency with higher scores.

### Frequency Score (FScore)

`ntile(Frequency, 5)`

Higher frequency → higher score.

### Monetary Score (MScore)

`ntile(Monetary, 5)`

Higher spend → higher score.

---

## 11.2.2 Composite RFM Score

Definition:

`RFMScore = RScore + FScore + MScore`

Range:

Minimum = 3  
Maximum = 15  

This composite value determines behavioral classification.

---

## 11.2.3 Segment Classification Logic

Customers are mapped into five behavioral groups:

- **Champions** → RFMScore ≥ 13  
- **Loyal Customers** → RFMScore ≥ 10  
- **Potential Loyalists** → RFMScore ≥ 7  
- **At Risk** → RFMScore ≥ 4  
- **Lost** → Remaining customers  

This classification converts numeric scoring into actionable strategy layers.

---

## Output Artifact

`rfm_scored_customers.rds`

This artifact contains:

- CustomerID
- Recency
- Frequency
- Monetary
- RScore
- FScore
- MScore
- RFMScore
- Segment

Purpose:

Transforms raw customer behavior into structured intelligence for downstream segment-level analytics and visualization.

---

# 11.3 Architectural Significance

The RFM Segmentation Engine represents the transition from transactional analytics to behavioral intelligence.

It enables:

- Targeted retention strategy
- Segment-specific campaign timing
- Revenue optimization by engagement depth
- Churn mitigation planning
- Behavioral personalization

This stage feeds directly into:

`10_segment_intelligence_engine.R`

Where segment-level structural, product, geographic, and lifetime metrics are evaluated.

The segmentation layer transforms the project from descriptive analytics into strategic customer intelligence architecture.  

# 12. Segment Intelligence Engine

This stage operationalizes segment-level behavioral intelligence.

It consumes:

`data/processed/rfm_scored_customers.rds`

and joins back to:

`data/processed/online_retail_enriched.rds`

This layer transforms static segmentation into actionable structural,
product, geographic, and financial intelligence.

All outputs are persisted using `saveRDS()`.

---

# 12.1 Segment Structural Metrics

This phase quantifies the structural weight and financial influence of each behavioral segment.

---

## 12.1.1 Customer Segment Distribution

Artifact:

`segment_customer_distribution.rds`

Definition:

Counts total customers within each segment.

Purpose:

Identifies dominant and underrepresented behavioral groups,
establishing population weight across segments.

---

## 12.1.2 Segment-Level Revenue Distribution

Artifact:

`segment_revenue_distribution.rds`

Metrics:

- TotalRevenue
- AverageRevenue

Purpose:

Differentiates:

- Absolute financial contribution
- Per-customer spending intensity

This distinguishes scale from efficiency.

---

## 12.1.3 Segment-Level Order Distribution

Artifact:

`segment_order_distribution.rds`

Metric:

- TotalOrderFrequency

Purpose:

Measures transactional concentration and engagement depth
within each segment.

---

# 12.2 Segment × Product Intelligence

This phase reconnects segment labels to transactional records,
enabling merchandise-level behavioral differentiation.

Join Logic:

`rfm_scored → enrich_retail_data`

Object:

`segment_product_data`

---

## 12.2.1 Top Bulk Purchase Products (Total Quantity)

Artifact:

`segment_product_total_quantity.rds`

Purpose:

Identifies dominant high-volume merchandise
within each behavioral segment.

---

## 12.2.2 Top Bulk Purchase Products (Average Quantity)

Artifact:

`segment_product_avg_quantity.rds`

Purpose:

Measures per-transaction purchase intensity,
distinguishing bulk behavior from frequency.

---

## 12.2.3 Top Revenue-Generating Products (Total Revenue)

Artifact:

`segment_product_total_revenue.rds`

Purpose:

Identifies products driving the highest monetary contribution
within each segment.

---

## 12.2.4 Top Revenue-Generating Products (Average Revenue)

Artifact:

`segment_product_avg_revenue.rds`

Purpose:

Evaluates revenue efficiency per transaction occurrence.

---

## 12.2.5 Top Frequently Ordered Products (Distinct Order Count)

Artifact:

`segment_product_order_count.rds`

Purpose:

Measures product penetration across distinct transactions
within each behavioral segment.

---

# 12.3 Segment × Geographic Intelligence

This phase evaluates geographic concentration patterns
within each behavioral segment.

---

## 12.3.1 Dominant Customer Countries (Population)

Artifact:

`segment_country_population_dominance.rds`

Purpose:

Identifies markets with highest customer density
within each segment.

---

## 12.3.2 High-Frequency Customer Countries

Artifact:

`segment_country_high_frequency.rds`

Purpose:

Identifies countries contributing the highest transaction frequency.

---

## 12.3.3 Revenue-Generating Countries (Total Revenue)

Artifact:

`segment_country_total_revenue.rds`

Purpose:

Determines geographic markets driving the largest
absolute monetary value.

---

## 12.3.4 Revenue-Generating Countries (Average Revenue)

Artifact:

`segment_country_avg_revenue.rds`

Purpose:

Measures revenue efficiency per transaction by geography.

---

## 12.3.5 Bulk Purchase Countries (Total Quantity)

Artifact:

`segment_country_total_quantity.rds`

Purpose:

Identifies countries driving the largest unit movement
within each segment.

---

## 12.3.6 Bulk Purchase Countries (Average Quantity)

Artifact:

`segment_country_avg_quantity.rds`

Purpose:

Evaluates per-transaction unit intensity across geographic markets.

---

# 12.4 Segment-Level Evaluation Metrics

This final phase transitions from descriptive segmentation
to long-term strategic evaluation.

---

## 12.4.1 Lifetime Value Evaluation (CLV)

Artifact:

`segment_lifetime_value_evaluation.rds`

Methodology:

CLV integrates:

- Average Order Value
- Purchase Frequency
- Engagement Lifespan

Purpose:

Estimates long-term monetary value of customers
within each segment.

This provides strategic prioritization guidance.

---

## 12.4.2 Churn Rate Assessment

Artifact:

`segment_churn_rate_assessment.rds`

Methodology:

Customers inactive beyond a defined threshold
are classified as "Churned."

Purpose:

Quantifies attrition risk within each segment,
enabling targeted retention strategy.

---

## 12.4.3 Average Order Value (AOV)

Artifact:

`segment_order_value_evaluation.rds`

Methodology:

Calculates revenue per completed order at the segment level.

Purpose:

Measures purchasing efficiency across segments, separating revenue scale from transaction quality.

---

# 12.5 Architectural Significance

The Segment Intelligence Engine represents the highest behavioral resolution within the analytical pipeline.

It enables:

- Strategic segment prioritization
- Revenue optimization by behavioral profile
- Geographic targeting precision
- Product-level personalization
- CLV-based resource allocation
- Churn mitigation planning

This stage transforms segmentation from classification into operational intelligence.

It completes the behavioral transformation of the project from transactional analytics to enterprise-grade customer strategy.  

# 13. Visualization Engine

The Visualization Engine is the terminal intelligence presentation layer of the analytical architecture.

This engine transforms processed analytical artifacts into:

• Programmatically generated visual outputs (R / ggplot2)  
• Interactive analytical dashboards (Tableau)  

All static visual artifacts are generated from persisted `.rds` objects and exported via `ggsave()` into structured `/visualizations/` directories.

All interactive behavioral dashboards are developed in Tableau, using exported analytical datasets derived from the processed layer.

This engine contains six architectural layers:

• Product Performance Visuals  
• Temporal Dynamics Visuals  
• Geographic Country Analysis Visuals  
• Cross-Dimensional Product Intelligence Visuals  
• Segment Intelligence Visuals  
• Advanced Behavioral Intelligence (Tableau Layer)

Figures 11.1 – 11.73 correspond to ggplot2 static outputs.

The Tableau Intelligence Layer extends analytical depth beyond static reporting.

---

# 13.1 Product Performance Visuals

Artifacts consumed:

- product_top_10_quantity_volume.rds  
- product_top_10_quantity_average.rds  
- product_top_10_revenue_volume.rds  
- product_top_10_revenue_average.rds  
- product_top_10_order_frequency.rds  

Figures generated:

- fig_11_1_top_bulk_quantity.png  
- fig_11_2_top_avg_quantity.png  
- fig_11_3_top_total_revenue.png  
- fig_11_4_top_avg_revenue.png  
- fig_11_5_top_order_frequency.png  

Purpose:

Visualizes merchandise dominance across:

- Total Quantity  
- Average Quantity  
- Total Revenue  
- Average Revenue  
- Order Frequency  

This layer converts numeric ranking into intuitive dominance comparison.

---

# 13.2 Temporal Dynamics Visuals

Temporal intelligence is structured into four sub-levels:

• Hour-Level  
• Day-Level  
• WeekPart-Level  
• Month-Level  

All artifacts originate from `/data/processed/temporal_*`.

Figures generated:

11.6 – 11.9  → Hour-Level  
11.10 – 11.15 → Day-Level  
11.16 – 11.20 → WeekPart-Level  
11.21 – 11.26 → Month-Level  

These visualizations reveal:

- Operational peak windows  
- Behavioral purchasing intensity  
- Revenue seasonality  
- Order throughput cycles  
- Weekday vs Weekend structural splits  

This layer supports workforce planning, inventory optimization, and promotional timing strategy.

---

# 13.3 Geographic Country Analysis Visuals

Artifacts consumed:

- country_top_10_total_quantity.rds  
- country_top_10_avg_quantity.rds  
- country_top_10_total_revenue.rds  
- country_top_10_avg_revenue.rds  
- country_top_10_total_orders.rds  
- country_top_10_total_order_lines.rds  

Figures generated:

11.27 – 11.34  

Visual output includes:

• Ranked bar charts  
• Geographic choropleth maps  
• Highlighted dominance labeling  

Purpose:

Transforms geographic performance metrics into spatial behavioral intelligence.

This supports international demand allocation and regional performance diagnostics.

---

# 13.4 Cross-Dimensional Product Intelligence Visuals

Artifacts consumed:

All `/data/processed/cross_*` objects.

Figures generated:

11.35 – 11.55  

Cross-dimensional axes include:

- Product × Hour  
- Product × Day  
- Product × WeekPart  
- Product × Month  
- Product × Country  
- Product × Revenue  
- Product × Order Frequency  

Purpose:

This layer exposes contextual merchandise dominance, revealing when, where, and under which behavioral structure products outperform.

It supports:

• Inventory forecasting  
• Time-based merchandising  
• Geographic assortment strategy  
• Revenue optimization timing  

---

# 13.5 Segment Intelligence Visuals

Artifacts consumed:

All `/data/processed/segment_*` objects.

Figures generated:

11.56 – 11.73  

This layer visualizes:

• Segment population dominance  
• Segment revenue concentration  
• Segment order distribution  
• Segment × Product heterogeneity  
• Segment × Country dominance  
• Segment CLV differentiation  
• Segment churn exposure  
• Segment AOV performance  

Purpose:

Converts segmentation analytics into executive-level decision intelligence.

It enables:

- Resource prioritization  
- Retention strategy calibration  
- Segment-based monetization strategy  
- Geographic behavioral clustering  

---

# 13.6 Advanced Behavioral Intelligence (Tableau Layer)

Beyond static visualization, advanced behavioral diagnostics were developed in Tableau to explore multi-variable interaction dynamics.

These dashboards are powered by exported processed artifacts and are not manually derived.

The Tableau analytical layer communicates the following intelligence:

---

## 13.6.1 Segment-Based Hourly Purchase Intensity

Analyzes purchasing intensity (Average Quantity)
across hourly cycles segmented by behavioral cluster.

Insight Objective:

• Identify segment-specific operational demand pressure  
• Detect time-sensitive high-value segment activity  
• Support staffing and fulfillment timing optimization  

---

## 13.6.2 Segment-Based Hourly Order Intensity

Evaluates transaction throughput by segment across the 24-hour cycle.

Insight Objective:

• Reveal high-frequency behavioral windows  
• Detect micro-cycle segment activation patterns  
• Inform system capacity planning  

---

## 13.6.3 Segment-Based Monthly Revenue Depth

Measures revenue contribution by segment across monthly cycles.

Insight Objective:

• Detect seasonal segment monetization strength  
• Identify segments driving peak revenue months  
• Support promotional alignment with behavioral value clusters  

---

## 13.6.4 Price–Demand Elasticity Relationships

Analyzes interaction between:

• Unit Price  
• Quantity Purchased  

Segmented by:

• Product  

Insight Objective:

• Detect elasticity sensitivity patterns   
• Support margin optimization strategy  

---

## 13.6.5 Recency–Monetary Behavioral Relationships

Explores relationship between:

• Time since last purchase  
• Total monetary contribution  

Insight Objective:

• Identify high-value customers approaching inactivity  
• Detect early churn risk among strong spenders  
• Prioritize proactive retention targeting  

---

## 13.6.6 Frequency–Monetary Behavioral Relationships

Analyzes the interaction between:

• Purchase Frequency  
• Total Spend  

Insight Objective:

• Detect loyalty monetization efficiency  
• Separate high-frequency low-value vs low-frequency high-value customers  
• Inform loyalty tier structuring  

---

# 13.7 Architectural Significance

The Visualization Engine now operates on two layers:

Layer 1 – Deterministic Static Intelligence (ggplot2)  
Layer 2 – Interactive Behavioral Diagnostics (Tableau)

Pipeline Flow:

Raw Data  
→ Cleaning  
→ Feature Engineering  
→ Foundational Metrics  
→ Product Intelligence  
→ Temporal Intelligence  
→ Geographic Intelligence  
→ Cross-Dimensional Intelligence  
→ RFM Segmentation  
→ Segment Intelligence  
→ Visualization Engine (R + Tableau)

This dual-system approach ensures:

• Deterministic auditability (R layer)  
• Exploratory depth (Tableau layer)  
• Executive interpretability  
• Operational applicability  

The Visualization Engine finalizes the transformation from transactional raw data into multi-dimensional behavioral intelligence.  

# 14. Reproducibility Protocol, Strategic Synthesis & Technical Closing Note

Section 14 formally concludes the analytical architecture.

It consolidates:

• Reproducibility guarantees  
• Strategic intelligence synthesis  
• Cross-platform integration validation  
• Technical closure documentation  

This ensures enterprise-grade auditability, scalability, and analytical integrity.

---

# 14.1 Reproducibility Protocol

The analytical system operates as a deterministic, artifact-driven architecture.

All analytical layers persist outputs as `.rds` objects within `/data/processed/`.

All ggplot2 visualizations are reproducibly regenerated from these artifacts.

All Tableau dashboards consume exported processed datasets derived strictly from these persisted objects.

No Tableau metric is independently calculated.

This guarantees:

• Cross-platform metric consistency  
• No computational divergence  
• Centralized artifact governance  

---

## 14.1.1 Deterministic Execution Flow

Sequential Execution:

1. Data Cleaning & Standardization  
2. Feature Engineering  
3. Foundational Metrics  
4. Product Intelligence  
5. Temporal Intelligence  
6. Geographic Intelligence  
7. Cross-Dimensional Intelligence  
8. RFM Segmentation Engine  
9. Segment Intelligence Engine  
10. Visualization Engine (R + Tableau)

Each stage depends exclusively on persisted artifacts.

No circular dependencies exist.

---

## 14.1.2 Environment Standardization

R Environment:

• R ≥ 4.2  
• tidyverse  
• ggplot2  
• ggrepel  
• sf  
• rnaturalearth  

Tableau Environment:

• Tableau Desktop / Tableau Public  
• Imported CSV exports from `/data/processed/`  
• No recalculated fields beyond presentation transformations  

---

## 14.1.3 Artifact Governance

Naming conventions enforce traceability:

Dimension Prefix  
Metric Descriptor  
Sequential Index  

This ensures:

• Audit clarity  
• Version control transparency  
• Platform synchronization  

---

# 14.2 Strategic Synthesis (Expanded)

The analytical system now integrates:

Static Intelligence  
+  
Interactive Behavioral Diagnostics  

The strategic hierarchy includes:

---

## 14.2.1 Merchandise Intelligence

High-volume vs high-margin differentiation.

---

## 14.2.2 Temporal Intelligence

Operational peak detection and seasonal revenue depth.

---

## 14.2.3 Geographic Intelligence

Spatial monetization and demand concentration.

---

## 14.2.4 Behavioral Segmentation Intelligence

Segment-level:

• Revenue depth  
• Order intensity  
• CLV differentiation  
• Churn exposure  

---

## 14.2.5 Behavioral Interaction Intelligence (Tableau Layer)

The advanced Tableau dashboards reveal:

• Segment × Hour behavioral micro-cycles  
• Segment × Month revenue depth  
• Price elasticity sensitivity patterns  
• Recency-driven churn exposure among high spenders  
• Frequency-driven monetization efficiency  

This layer moves beyond descriptive analytics into behavioral interaction diagnostics.

---

# 14.3 Architectural Integrity Summary

The analytical architecture is now:

Dual-layer visualization compliant 
Cross-platform synchronized  
Deterministically reproducible  
Behaviorally interpretable  
Deployment-ready  

It integrates:

Statistical R pipeline  
+  
Interactive Tableau intelligence  

Without metric divergence.

---

# 14.4 Technical Closing Note

The Online Retail Behavioral Analysis system transforms transactional retail data into:

• Multi-dimensional performance intelligence  
• Segment-based behavioral modeling  
• Geographic monetization diagnostics  
• Temporal demand forecasting signals  
• Elasticity-driven pricing intelligence  
• Churn exposure diagnostics  

The system is:

Modular  
Artifact-governed  
Cross-platform consistent  
Strategically extensible  

All components are reproducible, auditable, and enterprise-ready.

End of Analytical Framework.
